metadata.title: Why the Prompt That Worked Is Already Lost
metadata.purpose: Prompts are institutional knowledge that lives in chat history — unindexed, unsearchable, subject to context rolloff. A prompt card converts a one-time success into a repeatable process, making prompt quality cumulative rather than random.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: INK = prompt card (findable, maintained, repeatable); CRIMSON = lost chat history (buried, unsearchable, starts from zero each time)
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO organizational knowledge management theory, NO comparison of specific prompt library tools, NO discussion of fine-tuning or model personalization. Mechanism only: chat history is unindexed; prompt card converts one-time success into repeatable asset.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The prompt that worked is buried in a chat window she will never find again. Three months later she has the same task. She starts from scratch. She runs five iterations. She never gets the output back to where it was.

card.copy: Why the prompt that worked is already lost
card.sub: successful prompts disappear — unless you save them

beats.narration_text: A policy analyst spent forty minutes iterating a prompt that produced a perfect executive summary. Three months later she has the same task. She tries to recreate the prompt from memory, runs five iterations, and never gets the output back to where it was. Her colleague with a prompt library types 'program summary' and retrieves a card in thirty seconds.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A policy analyst spent forty minutes iterating a prompt that produced a perfect executive summary. Three months later she has the same task. She tries to recrea

beats.narration_text: The analyst used a prompt that worked perfectly. Three months later she could not recreate it. Why does a successful prompt disappear?

card.copy: Why does a successful prompt disappear?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The analyst used a prompt that worked perfectly. Three months later she could not recreate it. Why does a successful prompt disappear?

beats.narration_text: A successful prompt lives in chat history. Chat history is unindexed, unsearchable, and subject to rolloff. The prompt that worked is mixed with hundreds of failed attempts, casual messages, and other work. There is no tag that marks it as the one that succeeded.

When she tries to recreate it from memory, she reconstructs an approximation — missing the constraints she added on iteration three, the example she included on iteration five, the freeze clause that prevented Claude from inventing claims. Those additions were what made it work. They are gone.

A prompt is not a sentence — it is a specification. The specification is built up across iterations: initial task, then constraints, then examples, then freeze clauses, then review criteria. The final version has five layers. Memory reconstructs one.

A prompt card converts that specification into an asset. Not just the prompt text — but the purpose, the inputs it needs, the constraints, the output format, the review criteria, and one worked example that shows what good output looks like.

card.copy: Prompt card = the specification, not just the text

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A prompt card converts that specification into an asset. Not just the prompt text — but the purpose, the inputs it needs, the constraints, the output format, th

beats.narration_text: The card has an ID, a purpose, the inputs it needs, the constraints that made it work, the output format, the review criteria, one worked example, and known failure modes. That is the difference between a chat message and a workflow asset.

This applies every time a prompt works. Every time you iterate to a good output, you have produced something repeatable — but only if you save it. Without saving, each task starts from zero. With a card, prompt quality becomes cumulative.

A grant writer produces a perfect program summary on her seventh chat iteration. Three months later she needs the same summary for a new funder. She spends ninety minutes re-iterating. She never gets back to the seventh-iteration quality. A colleague types 'program summary' and retrieves a card in thirty seconds — the card carries the constraints and the example.

The card the colleague retrieves has: purpose — executive summary for program funders; inputs — program name, target audience, three measurable outcomes; constraints — 200 words, no invented claims, plain language; review criteria — outcomes must be specific and named. That is the seventh-iteration knowledge, preserved.

The practice: when a prompt produces output you would use again, save it immediately. Write four things — the purpose in one sentence, the inputs it requires, the constraints that made it work, and one review criterion you actually checked. That is a minimal card. That is enough to start from the seventh iteration instead of the first.
Prompts are specifications. Specifications are assets. Assets need to be saved, indexed, and maintained. The prompt that worked is only lost if you let it disappear into chat history.

card.copy: Don't start from zero next time.
card.sub: Save the specification. Build the library.
